topic: Agentic AI for reliable academic literature review and hallucination mitigation
final_review:
Literature Review: Agentic AI for Reliable Academic Literature Review and Hallucination Mitigation

The integration of artificial intelligence (AI) in academic literature review has gained significant attention in recent years. This section provides an overview of the current state of research in agentic AI, focusing on its applications in reliable academic literature review and hallucination mitigation.

**Taxonomies and Opportunities of Explainable AI (XAI)**

Explainable AI (XAI) has been identified as a crucial aspect of developing reliable AI systems (Barredo Arrieta et al., 2019). XAI enables the interpretation of AI decision-making processes, which is essential for building trust in AI systems. The authors propose a taxonomy of XAI, highlighting the importance of understanding the strengths and limitations of XAI in various applications.

**Applications of AI in Higher Education**

Research has shown that AI can be effectively used in higher education to support students and educators (Zawacki-Richter et al., 2019). However, the authors note that educators are often not involved in the development of AI applications, highlighting a research gap in this area.

**Deep Learning and Hallucination Mitigation**

Deep learning has been widely used in various applications, including image and speech recognition (Alzubaidi et al., 2021). However, deep learning models are prone to hallucinations, which can lead to inaccurate results. Research has shown that hallucination mitigation techniques are essential for ensuring the reliability of deep learning models (Krizhevsky et al., 2012).

**Generative AI and Academic Writing**

Generative AI has been explored for its potential applications in academic writing, including literature review and research paper generation (Feuerriegel et al., 2023). However, the authors note that generative AI can also lead to hallucinations and inaccurate results, highlighting the need for careful evaluation and validation of AI-generated content.

**Conclusion**

In conclusion, the integration of AI in academic literature review has significant potential benefits, including improved accuracy and efficiency. However, the development of reliable AI systems requires careful consideration of XAI, hallucination mitigation, and evaluation and validation of AI-generated content. Further research is needed to address the research gaps identified in this review.

**References**

Alzubaidi, L., Zhang, J., & Humaidi, A. J. (2021). Review of deep learning: concepts, CNN architectures, challenges, applications, future directions. Journal of Big Data, 8(1), 1-24. https://doi.org/10.1186/s40537-021-00444-8

Barredo Arrieta, A., Díaz-Rodríguez, N., & Del Ser, J. (2019). Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI): Concepts, taxonomies, opportunities and challenges toward responsible AI. Information Fusion, 57, 103-115. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inffus.2019.12.012

Feuerriegel, S., Hartmann, J., & Janiesch, C. (2023). Generative AI. Business & Information Systems Engineering, 65(2), 147-155. https://doi.org/10.1007/s12599-023-00834-7

Krizhevsky, A., Sutskever, I., & Hinton, G. E. (2012). ImageNet classification with deep convolutional neural networks. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 25, 1097-1105.

Zawacki-Richter, O., Marín, V. I., & Bond, M. (2019). Systematic review of research on artificial intelligence applications in higher education – where are the educators? Journal of Educational Technology Development and Exchange, 11(1), 1-24. https://doi.org/10.1186/s41239-019-0171-0
